article_meta.title: Christmas travelers left stranded as airports see mass flight cancelations

transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Analyze the article and return biased phrases, their bias types, and explanations. IMPORTANT ignore everything that quotes other people, only use what author is saying.CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences. If no bias is found, output an empty array: [] Format:[{"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}, {"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}] Example:[{"biasType": "Mind reading", "biasedText": "In a statement, Pillen characterized the NSEA's petition as an attack on children seeking the best education.", "explanation": "Assumes Pillen's view reflects the NSEA's intentions."}]

Types of media bias:- Spin: Vague or sensational language, including substituting an interpretive verb for a neutral one. Example: Writing "Senator X gloated over the bill's passage" rather than "said" or "responded" — the word 'gloated' implies smug pleasure without evidence, in the author's own voice.- Unsubstantiated claims: Claims without evidence.- Opinion Statements Presented as Fact: Subjective judgments presented as objective truth, without attribution. Example: A reporter writes in their own voice "The tax cuts failed to stimulate economic growth" — whether a policy "failed" is a contested  causal judgment that belongs in analysis, not unattributed news copy. - Sensationalism and Emotionalism: Information presented to shock.- Mudslinging/Ad Hominem: Unfair insults or attacks on character.- Mind reading: Assuming knowledge of others' thoughts.- Slant: Telling only part of the story present in the article — where information exists in the article but is structurally de-emphasized.   Example: An article reports a protester was injured prominently in the opening paragraphs, but mentions that three officers were also treated for injuries only in a single sentence near the end, attributed to a police statement. Both facts appear, but unequal prominence shapes the reader's impression. - Bias by Omission: Omitting information that would support an alternative viewpoint entirely — where the alternative perspective is absent from the article, not merely de-emphasized.   Example: An article on immigration enforcement quotes only affected families and advocacy organizations. No law enforcement perspective, border security rationale, or policy justification appears anywhere in the piece — the alternative viewpoint does not exist in the article at all. - Subjective Qualifying Adjectives: Bias revealed through adjectives.- Word Choice: Politically loaded words.- Negativity Bias: Emphasizing negative news.- Elite vs. Populist Bias: Deferring to prestigious institutions vs. dissenting perspectives. Exclude quotes from analysis. Be cautious and humble in your analysis. Present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones. CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: Christmas travelers left stranded as airports see mass flight cancelations
SOURCE: Fox Business

ARTICLE:

Travel Published December 27, 2025 8:38am EST Christmas travelers left stranded as airports see mass flight cancelations, delays JFK tops misery map with 77 cancellations as New Jersey, New York declare emergencies Facebook Twitter Threads Comments Print Email Add Fox Business on Google By Rachel Wolf FOXBusiness close video FOX Business Flash top headlines for December 26 Check out what's clicking on FoxBusiness.com. A winter storm on the East Coast has left post-Christmas travelers in a ho-ho-horrible position as airports see mass cancellations and delays. The website FlightAware showed that as of 7:50 a.m. ET on Saturday, 892 flights within, into or out of the U.S. were delayed and 637 were canceled. The website's "Misery Map" shows New York area airports, such as John F. Kennedy International Airport, LaGuardia Airport and Newark Liberty International Airport, experiencing the brunt of the travel chaos. HOLIDAY TRAVELERS FACE FLIGHT DELAYS AS FAA RESTRICTS AIRSPACE NATIONWIDE AMID WINTER WEATHER: REPORT Travelers at LaGuardia Airport (LGA) in the Queens borough of New York on Friday, Dec. 26, 2025. (Michael Nagle/Bloomberg via Getty Images / Getty Images)New Jersey and parts of New York are under state of emergency announcements as over 50 million Americans are set to be impacted by the winter storm barreling through the northeast, Fox Weather reported. Officials in New Jersey, New York, Connecticut and Pennsylvania are urging travelers and residents to exercise caution during the storm, according to Fox Weather. Additionally, a flurry of winter weather alerts have been issued in areas from New York City to Philadelphia.As of 7:52 a.m. ET, JFK Airport, which topped FlightAware's Misery Map list, had seen 30 delays and 77 cancellations since 5:00 a.m. ET. The Federal Aviation Administration said that flights arriving at the airport had been delayed by, on average, two hours and 37 minutes.Saturday's delays and cancellations were preceded by similar travel chaos on Friday as the northeast braced for the storm. On Friday, a total of 8,816 flights within, into or out of the U.S. had been delayed and 1,710 had been canceled, according to FlightAware. A traveler looks at a departures board in Terminal B at LaGuardia Airport (LGA) in the Queens borough of New York on Friday, Dec. 26, 2025. (Michael Nagle/Bloomberg via Getty Images / Getty Images)SOUTHWEST OFFERING $67 FLIGHTS IN NOD TO VIRAL INTERNET MEME: 'TRENDY' SALE"I came to New York to have a 'Home Alone' Christmas just by myself," Danniel Sermone, who was stuck at JFK International Airport, told the New York Post on Friday. "I wanted to hang out. I went shopping, and went to Times Square, crossed up there for a while with the big crowd. My next available flight is at 6 a.m. on the 28th. So I have been hanging out at JFK for about eight hours and still got like 30 left." Travelers arrive to catch flights at O'Hare International Airport on what is expected to be the busiest day of the holiday season on Dec. 21, 2025, in Chicago. (Scott Olson/Getty Images / Getty Images)GET FOX BUSINESS ON THE GO BY CLICKING HEREThe west coast was not spared from the winter weather travel chaos as it experienced a disastrous rain storm, though it did not appear to cause the same number of disruptions. FlightAware showed that Los Angeles International Airport had seen 64 flight delays and 15 cancellations by 4:10 a.m. PST. Additionally, FlightAware showed that as of 4:10 a.m. PST Harry Reid International Airport had seen 16 delays and nine cancellations. U.S. Stock Market Quotes Arrives Weekly Get Our Newsletter Get a brief on the top business stories of the week, plus CEO interviews, market updates, tech and money news that matters to you. We've added you to our mailing list.
---

parsed_output.explanation: The author uses cutesy, sensationalized language ('ho-ho-horrible') in their own voice to describe travelers' situations, adding emotional color rather than neutral reporting.

The word 'mass' in the headline is sensational framing by the author. While 637 cancellations is significant, 'mass' is a subjective, emotionally loaded term designed to shock rather than inform neutrally.

While 'Misery Map' is FlightAware's own branding, the author adopts and amplifies this emotionally loaded language without quotes or distancing language, making it their own characterization of the situation.

The author repeatedly uses the word 'chaos' in their own voice to characterize the situation, which is an emotionally loaded, subjective term rather than neutral description of delays and cancellations.
